---
title: "Topic 16: Health"
subtitle: "Stress, Coping, Behavior, and Evidence"
author: "Kristen"
date: "2026-03-24"
origin: "chat"
format:
  pptx:
    slide-number: true
execute:
  echo: false
---

## Topic 16: Health

::: notes
Talk track
- Today we are using health psychology to explain why the same stressful life conditions can lead to different patterns of coping, behavior, and health.

Transition
- Start with why this matters before we build the model.
:::

## Why this matters

- The same stressful week can lead to very different outcomes.
- One person stabilizes.
- Another spirals.

::: notes
Talk track
- Students already know that stress can feel bad. The more useful question is why the same kind of stress can produce different outcomes across people and across time.
- This lesson is about explaining that difference with a psychological model rather than with vague advice.

Example bank
- Two students have the same deadlines. One asks for help, sleeps enough, and recovers. The other stays stuck, sleeps badly, and starts skipping routines that usually help.
- Two patients receive the same diagnosis. One has stable support and flexible time. The other has transportation barriers and high stress at home.
- Two people both know sleep matters. One has stable habits and a predictable schedule. The other has night shifts, stress cues, and late-night scrolling loops.

Transition
- Now make the targets visible so students know what they are trying to learn.
:::

## Learning targets

- Explain stress as a process involving appraisal, body response, coping, and outcomes.
- Compare coping responses by fit and by later cost.
- Apply the model to a realistic stress-and-health situation.
- Evaluate a simple health claim more carefully.

::: notes
Talk track
- These targets tell students that today is not just vocabulary. They will explain, compare, apply, and evaluate.

Transition
- Next, give a quick roadmap so the lesson has visible structure.
:::

# Roadmap and context

## Today’s roadmap

- Health psychology and the biopsychosocial model
- Stress over time
- Coping, support, and health behavior
- Better questions for health claims

::: notes
Talk track
- This is the map for the class. We will start with the frame, move into stress, then coping and behavior, and end with evidence evaluation.

Transition
- Start the frame with the core claim of health psychology.
:::

## Health psychology uses a biopsychosocial model

- Health and illness reflect:
  - biology
  - behavior
  - thoughts and emotions
  - social conditions

::: notes
Talk track
- Health psychology does not reduce health to the body alone and it does not reduce health to mindset alone. The model is biopsychosocial because outcomes reflect multiple interacting layers.

Example bank
- Sleep problems can involve stress, habits, schedule, environment, and body-level arousal.
- High blood pressure can involve biology, chronic strain, activity, nutrition, medication use, and access to care.
- Pain can involve tissue damage, fear, expectation, attention, stress, and support.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if health is biopsychosocial, biology matters less.
- Correction: biology still matters. The model says biology is one part of a larger causal picture.

Transition
- Move from the broad frame to a short problem students can think about before more explanation.
:::

# Retrieval or warm-up

## Warm-up

Two students have the same bad week:

- too much work
- not enough sleep
- no extra time

By Friday:

- one is stressed but functioning
- one is stressed and unraveling

What would psychology look at first to explain the difference?

::: notes
Talk track
- Give students brief individual think time first, then partner talk.
- Listen for appraisal, control, coping, support, habits, barriers, and time under strain.

Debrief script
- If students say personality, push for mechanism. Ask what changed in the process.
- If students say stress level, ask what might have changed that level.

Transition
- Now turn that puzzle into the core stress claim for the lesson.
:::

# Concept build

## Stress is not only a feeling

- Stress is a body-and-mind response to demands.

::: notes
Talk track
- Students often treat stress as just a feeling word. Health psychology treats it as a response pattern that includes appraisal, body activation, coping, and later outcomes.

Example bank
- You notice a message from a professor and your attention narrows immediately.
- You hear bad news and your body reacts before you have a full plan.
- You stay keyed up for hours after a stressful event because the system did not shut down quickly.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: stress is only whatever someone reports feeling.
- Correction: subjective feeling matters, but stress also involves body response and behavior over time.

Transition
- Once stress is framed as a response, the next distinction is short-term versus chronic activation.
:::

## Short-term and chronic stress are different

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Short-term stress

- can support action
- brief activation
- recovery matters
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Chronic stress

- repeated or sustained activation
- harder recovery
- greater long-term strain
:::

:::

::: notes
Talk track
- Short-term stress can help people mobilize for a challenge. Chronic stress is different because the system stays activated or gets repeatedly reactivated.

Example bank
- One deadline this afternoon versus nonstop overload for weeks.
- A single public-speaking moment versus ongoing caregiving stress.
- One difficult exam versus living with unstable housing and daily uncertainty.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: all stress is harmful.
- Correction: short-term activation can be useful. The problem is repeated, prolonged, or poorly recovered activation.

Transition
- Students now need the main levers that help explain why stress outcomes differ.
:::

## What changes stress outcomes

- appraisal
- perceived control
- available resources
- time under strain

::: notes
Talk track
- These are four major levers in the stress process. They do not explain everything, but they help explain why the same demand can land differently.

Example bank
- A message from a professor can feel manageable or threatening.
- A problem with a clear next step feels different from a problem with no clear action.
- A student with social support has more usable resources than a student facing the same week alone.
- One rough day is not the same as months of overload.

Transition
- Start with appraisal because it is often the first psychological shift in the chain.
:::

## Appraisal

- What does this mean?
- How much control do I have?
- What resources can I use?

::: notes
Talk track
- Appraisal is the interpretation step. Students do not need a jargon-heavy definition. They need to understand that appraisal shapes how the demand is read.

Example bank
- “Can we talk after class?” can be interpreted as manageable or as a threat.
- A hard class can be read as a challenge or as proof that failure is coming.
- A new symptom can be read as annoying, scary, or urgent.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: appraisal is just positive thinking.
- Correction: appraisal is not denial. It is the way a person interprets meaning, control, and resources.

Transition
- Perceived control is important enough to separate from appraisal for one slide.
:::

## Perceived control matters

- Control changes stress intensity.
- Control changes coping options.

::: notes
Talk track
- People do not need full control for control to matter. Even partial control can change what happens next.

Example bank
- You can email a professor and make a plan.
- You cannot change that a family member is in the hospital today.
- You can control bedtime cues more than you can control your exam schedule.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if you cannot control everything, control does not matter.
- Correction: even partial control can reduce strain and expand coping choices.

Transition
- Check this distinction before adding body-level material.
:::

## Quick Check

Which situation gives a person more usable control right now?

- a missed deadline that can still be addressed
- a medical update that cannot be changed today

::: notes
Correct answer
- The missed deadline usually offers more immediate control.

Common wrong answers
- Wrong pattern: choosing the medical update because it feels more important.
- Why it fails: importance and controllability are not the same thing.

Debrief script
- Control is about what can be changed or acted on, not about how serious the situation feels.

Transition
- Now connect appraisal and control to the body response.
:::

## Why the body matters

- Stress affects action now.
- Stress can also create strain over time.

::: notes
Talk track
- This slide is a bridge. Students do not need a physiology lecture. They need a reason that body-level material belongs in a health lesson.

Transition
- Compare the two broad pathway patterns next.
:::

## Fast and slower stress pathways

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Sympathetic pathway

- faster activation
- prepares immediate action
- short time scale
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### HPA axis

- slower stress pathway
- involves cortisol
- longer time scale
:::

:::

::: notes
Talk track
- Keep this simple. Students need the contrast, not a memorized anatomy chain.
- The sympathetic pathway is the quick mobilization pattern. The HPA axis is a slower hormonal pathway that helps regulate stress over time.

Example bank
- Sudden alarm response versus staying activated through an ongoing stressful week.
- Immediate jolt before a presentation versus slower wear during ongoing overload.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: these are separate from psychology.
- Correction: these are part of the stress process that psychology is trying to explain.

Transition
- Cortisol is the part most likely to be misunderstood, so correct that immediately.
:::

## Cortisol needs context

- Cortisol is part of stress response.
- It is not a simple stress meter.

::: notes
Talk track
- Students often learn cortisol as if one number gives a complete answer. That is too simple. Timing matters, context matters, and one reading does not explain the whole stress story.

Example bank
- Morning and evening readings are not interchangeable.
- A stressful exam week is not the same as chronic strain over months.
- One lab measure does not explain someone’s whole health pattern.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: high cortisol always means more stress and low cortisol always means less stress.
- Correction: interpretation depends on timing, context, and the broader pattern.

Transition
- Move from one signal to the bigger long-term idea.
:::

## Chronic activation can create wear

- repeated activation
- harder recovery
- cumulative wear over time

::: notes
Talk track
- This is the student-facing version of allostatic load. The key idea is repeated strain across time, not one dramatic moment.

Example bank
- One rough day versus weeks of bad sleep and nonstop overload.
- Daily stress plus limited recovery time.
- Chronic strain plus low support and few options.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: one bad event explains long-term wear.
- Correction: repeated activation and limited recovery are the main idea.

Transition
- Now connect stress to health outcomes without overstating the claim.
:::

## Stress and health links are real but complex

- Stress can shape:
  - sleep and recovery
  - attention and decisions
  - health behavior
  - immune-related outcomes

::: notes
Talk track
- The point is not that stress automatically causes a specific disease. The point is that stress can influence multiple pathways that matter for health.

Example bank
- Poor sleep after chronic strain
- More avoidance or substance use during overload
- Harder recovery when stress and barriers pile up

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if stress affects health, stress directly causes disease in a simple one-step way.
- Correction: the links are real, but they are multi-step and multi-factor.

Transition
- Once the strain starts, coping helps determine the next part of the path.
:::

## Coping changes the path

- Coping can change:
  - the problem
  - the emotional impact
  - what happens next

::: notes
Talk track
- Coping is not one thing. It changes the situation, the experience, or the downstream consequences.

Example bank
- Making a plan
- Calming down enough to think clearly
- Avoiding a task and creating later cost

Transition
- Start with the kind of coping that tries to reduce the problem itself.
:::

## Problem-focused coping

- reduce the problem
- best when something can change

::: notes
Talk track
- Problem-focused coping is a good fit when part of the situation is controllable.

Example bank
- Email the professor.
- Break the task into steps.
- Ask a friend for help with transportation.
- Reduce task load for the week.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: problem-focused coping is always best.
- Correction: it depends on whether the situation can actually be changed.

Transition
- Now contrast that with coping that reduces emotional impact.
:::

## Emotion-focused coping

- reduce distress
- best when the stressor cannot change right now

::: notes
Talk track
- Emotion-focused coping is not automatically avoidance. It can be necessary when the situation cannot be fixed immediately and a person still needs to function.

Example bank
- Acceptance during an uncontrollable situation
- Reappraisal before acting
- Calming strategies that reduce panic enough to think clearly

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: emotion-focused coping is weak or irrational.
- Correction: it can be highly effective when the stressor is not currently controllable.

Transition
- Add one more layer because some coping feels good now but creates later problems.
:::

## Some coping helps now but costs later

- short-term relief
- long-term cost

::: notes
Talk track
- This is a major reality check for students. Some responses reduce distress in the moment but create worse outcomes later.

Example bank
- Procrastination
- Numbing with substances
- Doomscrolling late at night
- Skipping sleep to keep working

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if it lowers stress right now, it is effective coping.
- Correction: some responses reduce distress now while increasing cost later.

Transition
- Use a quick check to sort these coping types before moving on.
:::

## Quick Check

One stressful situation, three responses:

- make a plan
- calm down before acting
- avoid it tonight and deal with it later

Which response:

- reduces the problem
- reduces the emotional impact
- may create later cost

::: notes
Correct answer
- Make a plan reduces the problem.
- Calm down before acting reduces the emotional impact.
- Avoid it tonight and deal with it later may create later cost.

Common wrong answers
- Wrong pattern: calling all three “good coping.”
- Why it fails: usefulness depends on fit and on later cost.

Debrief script
- Ask which response is best only after asking what kind of situation it is and what cost it creates later.

Transition
- Next, widen the lens from coping inside the person to support and life context around the person.
:::

## Support, control, and context change outcomes

- social support
- perceived control
- life context

::: notes
Talk track
- These factors can buffer strain, expand options, and change how hard it is for chronic stress to affect the body and behavior over time.

Example bank
- A ride to the clinic changes adherence.
- Flexible work hours change sleep and recovery.
- A supportive friend changes coping options during overload.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: support helps everyone in exactly the same way.
- Correction: support helps through different pathways and does not work identically in every case.

Transition
- Now apply that logic to everyday health behavior.
:::

## Health behavior is not just willpower

- behavior reflects:
  - learning
  - environment
  - habit loops
  - stress

::: notes
Talk track
- Students often moralize health behavior. This slide corrects that. Knowledge matters, but behavior is shaped by cues, routines, barriers, stress, and context.

Example bank
- Knowing sleep matters but getting pulled into a late-night cue-routine loop
- Knowing medication matters but missing doses during overload
- Wanting to exercise but lacking time, safety, or transportation

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if people know better, they should do better.
- Correction: behavior is shaped by much more than knowledge or willpower.

Transition
- Show one simple mechanism with a concrete habit example.
:::

## Habit loop example

- cue
- routine
- consequence

::: notes
Talk track
- Keep this simple and concrete. A habit loop is useful here because it explains how stress and environment shape repeated behavior.

Example bank
- Cue: stress at night. Routine: endless scrolling. Consequence: short-term relief and worse sleep.
- Cue: stress after class. Routine: quick walk and breathing. Consequence: less arousal and clearer thinking.
- Cue: medication near the coffee maker. Routine: take medication. Consequence: more stable adherence.

Transition
- Move from everyday routines to the formal health-behavior term students may encounter.
:::

## Adherence over time

- Adherence is shaped by:
  - stress
  - barriers
  - memory
  - support
  - routine

::: notes
Talk track
- Adherence means following a treatment or health plan over time. It is not just a willpower issue. It reflects barriers, overload, support, and habit structure.

Example bank
- Missing medication during a chaotic week
- Missing physical therapy when transportation falls through
- Skipping appointments because time and stress pile up

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: nonadherence means the person does not care.
- Correction: nonadherence often reflects strain, barriers, forgetfulness, cost, or unstable routines.

Transition
- Now add one belief-related example that also teaches scientific caution.
:::

## Beliefs can change symptoms and outcomes

- expectation matters
- placebo effects are real

::: notes
Talk track
- Placebo and expectation effects show that beliefs can change symptoms and outcomes. This does not mean symptoms are fake. It means expectation can shape experience and response.

Example bank
- People report less pain when they expect a treatment to help.
- Expectation can change symptom perception and comfort.
- Belief can alter outcomes without proving that every treatment claim is valid.

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: placebo means it was all in someone’s head.
- Correction: placebo effects are real effects of expectation and context, not proof that symptoms were imaginary.

Transition
- That is exactly why strong health claims need strong evidence.
:::

## Why health claims need controls

- good comparisons matter
- replication matters

::: notes
Talk track
- Expectation effects are one reason controlled studies matter. Without controls, improvement can be misread as proof of a treatment effect when other explanations are possible.

Example bank
- People improve because they expected improvement.
- People improve because symptoms naturally fluctuate.
- People improve because the comparison group was different at baseline.

Transition
- End the concept build with a short framework students can actually use on health headlines.
:::

## How to evaluate a health headline

Ask:

- correlation or causation
- effect size
- confounds addressed

::: notes
Talk track
- This is the scientific-literacy payoff. Students should learn a short set of questions they can actually use outside class.

Example bank
- Did the study show a link or test a cause?
- Was the effect tiny or meaningful?
- Did the study address access, baseline risk, and stress exposure?

Common confusion
- Wrong idea: if a headline says “linked to,” it proved cause.
- Correction: correlation is not causation, and even good studies still need careful interpretation.

Transition
- Now students are ready to apply the full model in a realistic scenario.
:::

# Student processing activity

## Processing scenario

A student is:

- working many hours
- behind on two assignments
- sleeping badly
- skipping routines that usually help

::: notes
Talk track
- This scenario is intentionally ordinary. The goal is not drama. The goal is to trace a realistic stress-and-health chain.

Transition
- Give the group a clear output structure next.
:::

## Group task

Identify:

- one biopsychosocial factor
- one likely appraisal
- one body-level stress effect
- one coping response
- one possible later cost
- one support or context factor
- one health behavior effect

::: notes
Talk track
- Students should produce one specific answer for each line, not a vague paragraph.
- Push for mechanism, not just labels.

Debrief script
- Ask groups to compare where they agreed and where their chains differed.

Transition
- After the activity, bring the class back together for comparison and correction.
:::

# Debrief and feedback

## Debrief

- What did your group predict first?
- Where did your chains differ?

::: notes
Talk track
- Use this to surface different interpretations before correcting them.
- Ask which part of the chain seemed most important in each group’s answer.

Transition
- Then correct the predictable errors directly.
:::

## Common mistakes to correct

- stress is not only a feeling
- relief now is not always better later
- health behavior is not just willpower
- beliefs matter, but that does not prove every claim
- support does not work the same way in every case

::: notes
Talk track
- These are the predictable misunderstandings for this lesson.
- Keep correction concise and tied to what students actually said.

Transition
- Close by pulling the model back together.
:::

# Synthesis or application

## Synthesis

- Health reflects body, behavior, mind, and context.
- Chronic strain changes the picture.
- Coping, support, and habits shape outcomes.
- Good health claims need good evidence.

::: notes
Talk track
- This slide is the lesson in compressed form. If students leave with four usable ideas, these are the four.

Transition
- Ask one final applied question before closing.
:::

## Applied question

Why can the same stressful life conditions produce different health outcomes?

::: notes
Talk track
- Ask for answers using the lesson model: appraisal, control, body response, coping, support, habit loops, and context.

Transition
- Now connect this lesson to the next topic.
:::

# Closure

## Bridge to Topic 17

- Health behavior unfolds over time.
- Motivation helps explain starting, persisting, and stopping.
- Next: needs, incentives, goals, and self-regulation

::: notes
Talk track
- This bridge works because today’s lesson showed that health behavior is not just knowledge. It is behavior over time under stress, cues, and constraints.

Transition
- End with an exit ticket that checks both content and evidence evaluation.
:::

# Exit ticket

## Exit ticket

- Which response best fits a controllable stressor?
- Which question best checks whether a headline proved cause?
- In 1 to 3 sentences, explain one way support, control, or context could change a health outcome during a stressful week.

::: notes
Correct answer
- A response that targets the problem best fits a controllable stressor.
- Asking whether the result is correlational or causal is the first step in checking a headline.

Common wrong answers
- Wrong pattern: choosing the response that feels best immediately without considering control or later cost.
- Why it fails: coping fit depends on what can change and what happens later.
- Wrong pattern: focusing only on whether the finding sounds believable.
- Why it fails: plausibility is not the same as evidence quality.

Debrief script
- Collect responses individually. Use them to see whether students can apply both the stress model and the evidence questions.
:::
